coredata.dc:description: Hard carbon (HC) is one of the most promising materials for the negative electrodes of lithium (Li)-ion batteries, as it shows unique electrochemical performance. HC has a structure with small crystalline size and a higher discharge (delithiation) capacity than graphite. However, because of its complex structure, the quantitative relationship between the discharge capacities and Li-ion storage sites was not clear though it is important to realize HC with higher capacity. In this study, heat-treatment temperature was adopted to control HC structure. The structural and compositional parameters of pitch-derived HC, such as the lattice constant, crystalline size, pore size, and hydrogen (H) content were closely analyzed. Then, the amounts of Li stored in pores and interlayer spaces, and at the edge H of HC were estimated as the discharge capacities for each site. The estimated discharge capacities corresponded to the experimental ones at 0–0.2 V, 0.2–0.9 V, and 0.9–2.5 V, respectively. Based on this estimation, it was suggested that the suitable pores for Li storage are relatively large, and especially are pores larger than 1.2 nm. The quantitative relationship between the discharge capacities and Li-ion storage sites revealed in this study will play a guiding role in improving the electrochemical properties of HC.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hard carbon (HC) is one of the most promising materials for the negative electrodes of lithium (Li)-ion batteries, as it shows unique electrochemical performance. HC has a structure with small crystalline size and a higher discharge (delithiation) capacity than graphite. However, because of its complex structure, the quantitative relationship between the discharge capacities and Li-ion storage sites was not clear though it is important to realize HC with higher capacity. In this study, heat-treatment temperature was adopted to control HC structure. The structural and compositional parameters of pitch-derived HC, such as the lattice constant, crystalline size, pore size, and hydrogen (H) content were closely analyzed. Then, the amounts of Li stored in pores and interlayer spaces, and at the edge H of HC were estimated as the discharge capacities for each site. The estimated discharge capacities corresponded to the experimental ones at 0–0.2 V, 0.2–0.9 V, and 0.9–2.5 V, respectively. Based on this estimation, it was suggested that the suitable pores for Li storage are relatively large, and especially are pores larger than 1.2 nm. The quantitative relationship between the discharge capacities and Li-ion storage sites revealed in this study will play a guiding role in improving the electrochemical properties of HC.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium ion batteries (LIBs) have high energy density and are already widely used in mobile devices such as cellar phones and laptop computers. In recent years, demand for LIBs as a power source for electric vehicles (EVs) is also rapidly expanding [1–3]. LIBs consist of a positive electrode, negative electrode, separator, and electrolyte solution, and are charged and discharged reversibly by moving Li ions back and forth between the positive and negative electrodes. Carbon materials are mainly used as the negative electrode. Although natural and artificial graphites are currently the mainstream for LIBs [4], hard carbon (HC), which offers excellent durability and unique Li-ion input/output characteristics [5], is also attracting great attention as an alternative material for the negative electrodes of LIBs in EV and energy storage applications.

Carbon materials can be categorized into graphitizable carbons and non-graphitizable carbons. As a characteristic of graphitizable carbons, crystallinity changes drastically depending on the heat-treatment temperature. Low graphitic carbons are obtained by heat treatment at low temperature, but high-temperature treatment enables the development of a highly graphitic structure of the derived carbons. The graphitic carbons (e.g. graphite) have highly stacked graphene layers of a hexagonal structure (P63/mmc) in the c-axis direction. Non-graphitizable carbons, on the other hand, afford low crystallinity of the derived carbon over a wide temperature range. Non-graphitizable carbons usually contain HC, although the two terms are sometime used as synonyms in the field of batteries. HC has a structure in which small units of graphene layers are randomly arranged and have minute spaces (pores) between them [6]. This makes it difficult to align and develop the graphene layers, giving rise to a relatively low graphitization degree in HC even when it is subjected to high-temperature heat treatment at 3000 °C. In contrast to graphite, whose interlayer space is 3.35 Å and expands to 3.70 Å upon intercalation of Li into the interlayer spaces [7], HC has larger interlayer spaces of approximately more than 3.7 Å [8]. Because of these structural characteristics, HC shows unique electrochemical performance when used as a negative electrode in LIBs.

In LIBs, graphite can store Li only in the interlayer spaces between graphene sheets. When graphite is fully charged, Li is stored in the center of the six-membered rings, existing in every other six-membered ring, so the intercalation compound can be described as LiC6 from its stoichiometry. The amount of Li per unit weight of carbon is calculated as 372 mAh/g, corresponding to the theoretical capacity of graphite. It is also well known that the electrode potential of graphite changes in a stepwise manner, depending on the state of charge, and its charge/discharge curves show plateau regions with constant potentials. In the graphite charging process, for example, the plateau regions are observed at about 0.21 V, 0.12 V, and 0.09 V vs Li/Li+, which correspond to the changes from Dilute Stage 1 to Stage 4, from Stage 2L (LiC18) to Stage 2 (LiC12), and from Stage 2 to Stage 1 (LiC6), respectively [9,10]. In contrast, HC can store Li not only in the graphene interlayer spaces but also in the pores which are an inherent feature of the material, and for this reason, the Li storage capacity of HC is higher than that of graphite. However, unlike graphite, the theoretical Li storage capacity of HC has not been estimated successfully to date due to its characteristic structure with small crystalline size. The correlation between the electrode potential and the state of Li storage in HC is also significantly different from that of graphite, in that the electrode potential of HC changes continuously, and there are no distinct plateau regions similar to the stage changes observed in graphite. Therefore, it was suggested that the continuous change in the electrochemical potential of HC may be related to its structure with small crystalline size, and thus, the existence of various Li storage sites.

Until now, a number of studies have attempted to explain the Li storage sites of HC in relation to its electrode potential [11–18]. For example, Park et al. divided the electrode potential of HC during discharge (delithiation) into ranges lower than 0.12 V as void-like spaces, 0.12–0.8 V as carbonaceous interlayers, and above 0.8 V as different types of voids with heteroatoms and/or dangling bonds [11]. Mochida et al. reported that in the process of discharge (delithiation), the Li storage site in the potential range of 0–0.13 V corresponds to micro-voids surrounded by hexagonal sheets, 0.13–0.25 V is attributed to intercalated Li, 0.25–0.8 V is due to Li charge-transferred with surface sites, and higher than 0.8 V is related to micro-spaces located at the edges of carbon clusters [12,13]. Dahn et al. measured the structural change of carbon by Li insertion and removal using X-ray diffraction and small angle X-ray scattering, and concluded that the potential range lower than 0.2 V corresponds to nanopores, the range higher than 0.2 V corresponds to interlayer intercalation, and that around 1 V of Li removal corresponds to residual hydrogen-terminating aromatic fragments [14,15]. Kubota et al. assigned the discharge (delithiation) potential range of 0.002–0.05 V to pseudo-metallic Li clustering in micropores, 0.05–0.25 V to Li intercalation into the narrow interlayer space, and 0.25–2.0 V to large interlayer spaces of defective carbon layers [16]. Summarizing these reports, it can be roughly stated that the low, middle, and high potential ranges correspond to pores, interlayer spaces, and other sites derived from the graphene edge, respectively.

Many researchers have also reported attempts to quantitatively estimate the amount of Li storage. For instance, Zheng et al. reported that the amount of hydrogen is proportional to the capacity in the 1 V plateau region during Li removal [19]. Matsumura et al. pointed out that a parameter related to the basal surface of graphitic crystalline shows a correlation with the total discharge (delithiation) capacity [20]. Kim et al. tried to calculate theoretical plateau (≈0 V) capacities for Li and Na ions from the density of HC [21]. Tokumitsu et al. estimated the individual amounts of Li in interlayers and in “cavities” and concluded that the sum of the estimated amounts is in good agreement with the total charge (lithiation) capacity [22]. However, since these reports considered only part or the total of the charge/discharge capacity, the capacity of each storage site in HC in the corresponding potential regions has not been elucidated. If the quantitative relationship between the discharge capacities and Li-ion storage sites were revealed and the critical structural parameters affecting discharge capacity were clarified, the guideline could be obtained to make discharge capacity of HC higher, resulting in reduction of battery weight and extension of battery life.

With this background, in this study, HC samples with varying structure were prepared by adopting heat-treatment temperature as parameter to control structure. The structure of HC was closely analyzed, and the relationship between the structure of HC and the amount of Li storage at each electrode potential was investigated. From these results, the capacity of each storage site of HC was quantitatively estimated and the effect of structural parameters were discussed.

Coal tar pitch (quinoline-insoluble content: 3 wt%, softening point: 81 °C) was used as the raw material of the HC samples. Intentional addition of elements such as S, P, N, etc. was not carried out. Air was blown in the molten coal tar pitch at 340 °C. The pitch was polymerized by the dehydration reaction, and air-blown pitch (softening point: 280 °C) was obtained. The solid air-blown pitch was then ground and oxidized at 260 °C for 5 h in an air flow. This is very important process to avoid remelting and to form the pore structure during next heat-treatment process [12,23,24]. The oxidized pitch was again ground and heat-treated at a certain temperature in a N2 atmosphere, and HC was obtained. The heat-treatment temperatures used in this step were in the range from 700 °C to 1200 °C. In this paper, a sample heat-treated at X °C is denoted HC-X, where X is the heat-treatment temperature (e.g. HC-700).

The elemental composition of the HC samples was measured by using an elemental analyzer (Yanaco CHN Corder MT-6; Yanaco Apparatus Development Laboratory Co., Ltd.). The oxygen content (Odiff.) was calculated by the following equation:
Odiff. [mass %] = 100 − (C + H + N).

XRD was measured by using an X-ray diffractometer (Ultima IV; Rigaku Corporation). The X-ray source was Cu-Kα, the measurement angle was from 3° to 90°, and the measurement step was 0.02°. 5 wt% of CeO2 (NIST) was mixed with the HC as an internal standard.

SAXS was measured at the Spring-8 Synchrotron Radiation Facility BL08B2. The measurement conditions were wavelength of X-ray: 1 Å (12.4 keV), detector: PILATUS3-S-1M, and camera length: 558 mm. The pore size distribution was analyzed by Nano-Solver (Rigaku Corporation), supposing that the pore shape was spherical, and the distribution was a gamma distribution.

A powder sample of about 4 mg was purged in He gas in a quartz tube, and the ESR spectrum was measured at 7 °C using EMXplus (Bruker Corporation). Copper (II) sulfate pentahydrate (NACALAI TESQUE, INC.) was used as a standard substance.

The sample powder was heated at a heating rate of 10 °C/min to 1500 °C in Ar gas at a flow rate of 200 mL/min by simultaneous thermogravimetry (STA 2500, NETZSCH Japan K.K.). Quantitative analyses were carried out by infrared absorption spectrometry for the emitted gases H2O, CO, and CO2 and with a quadrupole mass analyzer for H2 and N2. Weight change of sample during heating was also measured by TG in Ar.

Apparent density was measured by the pycnometer method according to the Japanese Industrial Standard JIS Z 8807:2012, Methods of measuring density and specific gravity of solid, using 1-butanol (FUJIFILM Wako Pure Chemical Corporation, density: 0.808 g/cm3) as a standard substance, at a measurement temperature of 25 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scanning electron microscopy and Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (SEM-EDS)
ce:section.ce:para: Surface appearance of HC powder was observed by scanning electron microscope FlexSEM 1000 II (Hitachi High-Tech Corporation). HC powder was loaded on carbon tape and observed at an accelerating voltage of 5 keV. Elemental composition was also measured by element energy dispersive x-ray spectroscopy (Element C2B, EDAX Inc.), at an accelerating voltage of 15 keV.

HC powder was coated by carbon deposition as protective coating and specimen was prepared by focused ion beam (FIB) and Ar milling, using SEM-FIB hybrid system SMI3050MS (Seiko Instruments Inc.) and Ar milling system Gentle Mill IV (Technoorg Linda Co. Ltd.). Bright field STEM image of specimen was observed by STM system JEM-ARM200F (JEOL Ltd.). Accelerating voltage was 200 keV. BF image was analyzed by Fast Fourier Transform (FFT).

Gas adsorption-desorption isotherm was measured by N2 at −196 °C (77.3 K) by Autosorb-1-MP (Quantachrome Corp.). From the results, pore distribution was calculated by density functional theory (DFT) method.
Gas adsorption-desorption isotherm was measured by CO2 at 25 °C by BELSORP-max-S (MicrotracBEL Corp.). From the results, pore distribution was calculated by DFT method.

After the electrochemical measurements, the cells were disassembled, and the negative electrode was ejected in a glovebox purged with Ar gas. The electrode was washed with a mixture of ethylene carbonate and diethyl carbonate (3 : 7, v/v) (KISHIDA CHEMICAL Co., Ltd.) and then, washed with diethyl carbonate (KISHIDA CHEMICAL Co., Ltd.) and dried. Then, the active material was peeled off from the copper foil and diluted with dried alumina, and the mixture was sealed in an NMR sample tube. The 7Li magic-angle spinning (MAS) NMR spectra were measured with an NMR system (400WB, Agilent Technologies, Inc.) at a MAS rate of 12–14 kHz at room temperature. 7Li MAS NMR measurements at room temperature and −90 °C were also carried out with an NMR spectrometer (JNM-ECZL500, JEOL Ltd.) at a MAS rate of 16 kHz. Lithium chloride (FUJIFILM Wako Pure Chemical Corporation) was dissolved in water at a concentration of 1 mol/dm3, and the solution was used as a reference standard for chemical shift (0 ppm).

After the electrochemical measurements, the specimens for 1H NMR were prepared by the same procedure as those for 7Li NMR. In this case, however, the 1H NMR spectra of HC-700 powder and the derived electrode were also measured. The 1H NMR spectra were acquired by using AVANCE III 600 (Bruker Corporation) at a MAS rate of 20 kHz at room temperature. Adamantan (KANTO CHEMICAL CO., INC.) was used as a reference standard for chemical shift (1.91 ppm).

Polyvinylidene difluoride (PVdF, KF Polymer L #9130, KUREHA CORPORATION) was used as a binder. The negative electrode compound paste was prepared by mixing HC and PVdF with a weight ratio of HC : PVdF = 96 : 4 in N-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone (FUJIFILM Wako Pure Chemical Corporation). The prepared paste was coated uniformly on copper foil (thickness: 15 μm) and dried at 100 °C in a blower dryer. Then, the negative electrode compound layer obtained in this manner was pressed, punched, and dried at 100 °C in a vacuum atmosphere for more than 8 h, and a negative electrode was obtained.

A half-cell (2 electrode HS cell) was prepared by assembling the negative electrode, electrolyte, separator (polyethylene, thickness: 20 μm), and metallic lithium of the counter electrode. The electrolyte was 1 mol/dm3 lithium hexafluorophosphate (LiPF6) in a mixture of ethylene carbonate and ethyl methyl carbonate (1 : 2, v/v) (KISHIDA CHEMICAL Co., Ltd.). The negative electrode size was 16 mmϕ and electrode loading was 10 mg/cm2. Apparent electrode density was 1.0 g/cm3.

The electrochemical measurement of the half-cell was conducted at 25 °C. In this study, lithiation process of HC is defined as “charge” and delithiation process is defined as “discharge” [11–13,16]. First, the cell was charged by the constant current (CC) method at a current density of 45 mA/g to a cell voltage of 1 mV. Next, the cell was continuously charged by the constant voltage (CV) method at a voltage of 1 mV to a current density of 1 mA/g. Then, the cell was discharged by the CC method at a current density of 45 mA/g to a cell voltage of 2.5 V.

The half-cell (2 electrode coin cell) for measurement of 7Li NMR and 1H NMR was prepared in the same way as described above. The negative electrode size was 14 mmϕ and electrode loading was 5 mg/cm2. Apparent electrode density was 1.0 g/cm3.

The electrochemical measurement was conducted at 25 °C. First, the cell was charged by the CC method at a current density of 130 mA/g to a cell voltage of 1 mV. Then, the cell was continuously charged by the CV method at a voltage of 1 mV to a current density of 2.6 mA/g. Finally, the cell was discharged by the CC method at a current density of 130 mA/g to a cell voltage of 2 V. Some measurements were stopped before charge or discharge was complete, the cells were disassembled, and the ejected negative electrodes were used to measure 7Li NMR and 1H NMR.

The results of the elemental analysis are shown in Table 1 . The amounts of H and Odiff. decreased as the heat-treatment temperature increased, indicating evolutions of heteroatoms during the carbonization of pitch at higher temperatures. In contrast, the amount of N slightly increased from HC-700 to HC-950 and decreased from HC-950 to HC-1100.

Fig. 1 (a) shows the results of the XRD measurement. Regardless of the heat-treatment temperature, the XRD patterns of the HCs did not show sharp peaks like those of graphite. Rather, broad peaks specific to HC were observed at around 24°, 44°, and 80°. The lattice constants in the c-axis direction, c 0, and the a-axis direction, a 0, were calculated by Bragg's equation from the position of the peak at 24° corresponding to diffraction by the (002) plane and the peak at 74°–80° corresponding to diffraction by the (110) plane, respectively [25]. The crystalline sizes along the stacking direction, L
c , and the layer sheet direction, L
a , were also calculated from the full width at the half maximum of the same peaks by Scherrer's equation [25]. The obtained values are shown in Table 2 . The c 0 values ranged from 7.49 Å to 7.52 Å, which were larger than the 6.71 Å of graphite, but slightly smaller than the reported value of 7.6 Å for HCs derived from resin or biomass [26–28]. As for a 0, the 2.41 Å obtained for all the HCs in this study was close to the 2.46 Å of graphite. While the c 0 and a 0 values hardly changed with the heat-treatment temperature, L
c and L
a increased slightly with an increase in the heat-treatment temperature. The number of carbon atoms in one graphene layer, which was calculated from L
a and a 0 [29], increased from 73 atoms to 115 atoms as the heat-treatment temperature increased. The number of stacking layers, calculated from L
c and c 0, also increased from 4.2 layers to 4.4 layers as the heat-treatment temperature increased. Table 2 also shows the apparent density estimated by the pycnometer method. An increase in the heat-treatment temperature gave rise to an increase in the apparent density. Assuming that the spaces where 1-butanol could not infiltrate were closed pores of HC, this result suggested that the amount of closed pores for 1-butanol decreased with increasing heat-treatment temperatures.

SAXS profiles for the prepared HCs are shown in Fig. 1(b). The SAXS profiles showed a broad peak at around 0.5 to 2 nm−1, and the peak intensity increased with the heat-treatment temperature. As shown in a previous report, this peak can be attributed to scattering from the pores of HC [30]. Fig. 1(c) shows the average diameter of the pores estimated from the SAXS data, supposing that the pore shape is spherical and the distribution is a gamma distribution. The average pore diameter increased as the heat-treatment temperature increased, as previously reported [16,22]. However, as mentioned in the previous section, the increase in the apparent density suggested a decrease in the absolute volume of pores with increasing heat-treatment temperatures (Table 2). Therefore, in order to estimate the amount of Li storage in pores, it is necessary to consider both the size and the volume of pores, which will be discussed in the following sections.

The amounts of radical for HC-700 and HC-1100 measured by ESR are shown in Fig. 1(d). The amount of radical for HC-700 was 1.12 × 1020 spin/g, and that for HC-1100 was 1.76 × 1019 spin/g. In the process of carbonization of pitch, it has been reported that the radical amount reaches its maximum around 700 °C and then decreases with increasing temperature above 700 °C [31]. The radical amounts obtained in this study were in close agreement with those in the previous report [31].

Because certain amounts of H were detected by the elemental analysis of HC-700 (Table 1), a TPD measurement was carried out to confirm the form of existence of H. Remarkable amounts of H2 were emitted in the temperature range from 700 °C to 1400 °C (Fig. 1(e)). In contrast, the emitted amounts of CO and CO2 were much smaller than that of H2. Duplicated multiple peaks of CO and CO2 were observed. The peaks of CO ranged from 500 °C to 1100 °C, and the peaks of CO2 ranged from 100 °C to 600 °C, indicating thermal decomposition of oxygen-containing functional groups. These results suggest that most of the hydrogen existed in the edge of aromatic rings as edge H [32,33].

TG curve of HC-700 is shown in Fig. 1(f). As a reference, the result of oxidized pitch is also shown in Fig. 1(f). The weight of HC-700 started to decrease at 700 °C and the weight loss at 900 °C reached about 1.4 %. After that, weight change became small once, and then, above 1100 °C, weight decreased again by about 1.2 % at 1500 °C. These results corresponded to the CO emission observed by TPD in Fig. 1(e).

Surface morphologies observed by SEM are shown in Fig. S1 in the Supplementary Data. Particle size and micro-morphology such as surface roughness didn't change by heat-treatment temperatures. EDS spectrum and elemental composition of HC-700 estimated by EDS are also shown in Fig. S2 and Table S1 in the Supplementary Data, respectively. Only peaks of C, O and S were observed in the EDS spectrum, though N peak around 0.4 keV may exist around shoulder of C peak. As elemental composition, 5.56 wt% of O and 0.54 wt% of S was detected. S is supposed to be derived from the impurity of pitch. From this result and elemental analysis data shown in Table 1, it was found that HC-700 contains C, H, O and small amount of N and S.

Cross sectional images observed by STEM are shown in Fig. S3 in the Supplementary Data. Features of small crystalline size for each sample were observed. At higher heat-treatment temperature, the winding carbon stacking sheets were more clearly observed and FFT pattern also became a little bit clearer. Pore structure was not observed because existence probability of pores is very low in this observation range.

Pore size distributions and pore volume estimated from N2 adsorption isotherms at −196 °C are shown in Fig. S4 and Table S2 in the Supplementary Data. In HC-700, large open pores whose size is more than 5 nm was observed. When heat-treated at higher than 800 °C, the amount of open pores which N2 gas can enter was very small. As for analysis of ultra-micropores, on the other hand, CO2 adsorption technique at 25 °C was applied. The peaks of pore distribution were observed around 0.35 nm and 0.5 nm in HC-700, HC-800, HC-850 and HC-900 (Fig. S5 in the Supplementary Data). In HC-950, the peak at 0.35 nm decreased and 0.5 nm increased. In HC-1000, the amount of open pores was very small. In HC-1100 and HC-1200, adsorption of CO2 cannot be observed.

To clarify the Li storage site, 7Li MAS NMR measurements were carried out. Fig. 2 (c) shows the 7Li MAS NMR spectra of HC-1100 at room temperature in the 1st cycle electrochemical measurement, together with the corresponding charge (lithiation) and discharge (delithiation) curves (Fig. 2(a) and (b)). Points A–D correspond to charge 50, 100, 200, and 300 mAh/g, respectively, and point E is 428 mAh/g (full charge). Points F–H correspond respectively to discharge 100, 200, and 300 mAh/g and point I is 336 mAh/g (full discharge). Up to 50 mAh/g, only a peak at 0 ppm was observed (point A). Then, with charging to 100 mAh/g, the peak at 2.2 ppm newly appeared (point B). Upon the further charging, this new peak shifted to the high frequency direction, and peaks were also found at 0 ppm (points B–D). The new peak which shifted with charging is attributed to the Li intercalated in the interlayer spaces, and the shift is presumed to be a result of an increase in the amount of interlayer Li. On the other hand, the peak at 0 ppm is due to Li in the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI). Because the area of the peak at 0 ppm did not show noticeable changes by charging, it is suggested that SEI formed in the early stage of charging, and the amount of SEI remained substantially constant after that stage. At the full charge of 428 mAh/g (point E), a broad peak was observed at 45.7 ppm. Because the peak shifted largely as compared with that at point D (300 mAh/g), it is suggested that the chemical state of Li changed. On discharging to 100 mAh/g (point F), the broad peak at 45.7 ppm disappeared and a sharp peak was observed at 14.9 ppm. After that, the peak at 14.9 ppm shifted to the lower frequency direction as discharging proceeded, and finally, only the peak of SEI near 0 ppm was observed in the fully discharged sample at 336 mAh/g (point I).

To investigate the remarkable peak shift at full charge in detail, 7Li MAS NMR was measured at low temperature (−90 °C), as this makes it possible to deconvolute a merged peak due to the Li site exchange. The results are shown in Fig. 2(d)–(f). In the sample charged to 200 mAh/g, as shown in Fig. 2(d), except for the peak of SEI near 0 ppm, only one peak at 11.8 ppm was observed at room temperature, and at −90 °C, that peak showed a small shift to the low frequency direction, but peak split was not observed. Upon full charging to 428 mAh/g, one peak at 42.6 ppm was observed at room temperature, but at −90 °C, the peak split into two, one at 17.7 ppm and another broad one at 120–190 ppm (Fig. 2(e)). The former peak was attributed to Li in the interlayer spaces and the latter at 140–180 ppm was assigned to Li clusters (pseudo-metal Li) in pores of HC [34,35]. Lithium metal peak at 263 ppm [35] was not observed. For the sample discharged to 100 mAh/g, as shown in Fig. 2(f), one peak at 15.8 ppm was observed both at room temperature and at −90 °C without peak splitting. These results suggest that, in HC-1100, Li was stored in pores at the end of charging, and was released from the pores at the beginning of discharging.

To compare the effect of the heat-treatment temperature on the Li storage site, 7Li MAS NMR measurements at room temperature were also carried out for HC-700. Sampling points are shown in Fig. 3 (a) and (b), and the result of 7Li MAS NMR is shown in Fig. 3(c). A peak shift to 10.5 ppm was also observed for HC-700 after full charge (point B). It is considered that this shift was attributed to Li storage in the interlayer spaces, but the shift of the NMR peak was smaller, even though the charge capacity was larger than that of HC-1100. That is, the ionicity of the Li stored in the interlayer spaces of HC-700 might be higher than that of HC-1100 [36,37]. Meanwhile, in the case of HC-700, a large peak shift due to pores was not observed even in the high charge state of 400–882 mAh/g (points A to B). This result suggests pores of HC-700 did not work as Li storage site. Because the pore size was small at low heat-treatment temperatures as shown in the SAXS results (Fig. 1(c)), it is thought that Li cannot be stored in small pores, which will be discussed in the following sections.

As one Li insertion model of carbons treated at low temperature, lithium-naphthalene complexes have been proposed [38]. In addition, as a result of NMR measurement of graphite, it has been reported that the peak at 20 ppm is attributable to lithium-naphthalenide [39]. In this study, however, no obvious peak at 20 ppm was detected in HC-700.

Many reports [13,19,38,40–43] have pointed out that the graphene edge is one of Li storage sites for HC heat-treated at low temperature. To investigate the interaction between Li and H, 1H NMR was conducted. Fig. 3(d) shows the 1H NMR spectra of HC-700. To distinguish H species in HC and PVdF binder, both the powder sample without binder and electrode (with binder) were measured. In the measurement of the powder sample, a broad peak was observed at 6.3 ppm. Because this peak was not observed in HC-1100 (Fig. S6 in the Supplementary Data), it is considered to be a unique peak of H in HC-700 containing 1.57 mass % of H. Judging from the chemical shift, the peak was attributed to H in the unsaturated hydrocarbon or -OH adjacent to unsaturated hydrocarbon. In contrast, the electrode sample before electrochemical measurement showed a sharp peak at 2.7 ppm in addition to the peak at 6.3 ppm. Judging from the peak position, this sharp peak was attributed to H in the saturated hydrocarbon derived from the PVdF binder [44]. At a charge of 400 mAh/g (point A), the peak position of the broad peak shifted to around 4.1 ppm, and in addition to this broad peak, new peaks were observed at 3.9 ppm and 4.8 ppm at a full charge of 882 mAh/g (point B). At a discharge of 200 mAh/g (point C), those new peaks disappeared, and a peak was found at around 4.1 ppm, similar to that at a charge of 400 mAh/g (point A). After full discharge (point D), the peak position of the broad peak shifted to around 5.0 ppm and the peak shape was similar to that before charging. The origin of these peak shifts and the new peaks in the 1H NMR spectra is not clear, but they may be due to the interaction between Li and H.

To investigate the effect of heat-treatment temperature on the charge/discharge curves in more detail, 1st cycle charge curves of HCs prepared at different heat-treatment temperatures are shown in Fig. 4 . To compare the details, Fig. S7 in the Supplementary Data also shows the 1st and 2nd cycle charge/discharge curves for each HC sample. The 1st and 2nd cycle charge/discharge capacities and Coulombic efficiency are also listed in Table S3 in the Supplementary Data. The charge curves in Fig. 4(a) showed the characteristic shape of HC with a rapid decrease in potential in the CC region and a long CV region. As the heat-treatment temperature increased, the charge capacity of the CC and CV regions decreased. In the discharge curves in Fig. 4(b), plateau regions were observed at around 0.1 V and around 1 V. The capacity of the plateau region at around 0.1 V increased and that at around 1 V decreased at higher heat-treatment temperatures. The total capacity decreased as the heat-treatment temperature increased.

To discuss the potential regions corresponding to the different Li storage, dQ/dV analysis were applied to the discharge curves, and the results are shown in Fig. 4(c). Because the charge curves involve the capacities due to decomposition of the electrolyte and SEI formation and it is difficult to analyze the CV region, here, we focused on the discharge capacity. The samples prepared at all heat-treatment temperatures displayed the above-mentioned peaks at around 0.1 V and around 1 V. These peaks corresponded to the plateau regions in Fig. 4(b). With an increase of the heat-treatment temperature, the peak at around 0.1 V became higher and that at around 1 V became lower. The shape around 0.2–0.9 V was similar regardless of the heat-treatment temperature. Based on these results, the reaction of delithiation of Li was divided into the three potential regions denoted here as 0–0.2 V, 0.2–0.9 V, and 0.9–2.5 V. The discharge capacities of these potential regions are shown in Fig. 4(d). The discharge capacity of the 0–0.2 V region increased with increasing heat-treatment temperature, but the discharge capacity of 0.2–0.9 V was less sensitive to temperature. In contrast, the discharge capacity of 0.9–2.5 V decreased remarkably with increasing temperature. The total discharge capacity decreased with an increase in the heat-treatment temperature due to the large impact of the capacity in the 0.9–2.5 V region. Most of the previous reports [11–18] have suggested that these low, middle, and high potential regions correspond to pores, interlayer spaces, and other sites derived from the graphene edge, respectively. However, some reports [45–47] have assigned low voltage capacity to interlayer spaces and high voltage capacity to adsorption to defects. In this study, the correspondence between voltage regions and Li storage sites was reexamined and the Li storage amounts in each site were estimated quantitatively in following sections.

As for the Coulombic efficiency in Table S3 in the Supplementary Data, the 1st cycle Coulombic efficiency increased with increasing heat-treatment temperature. This might be because surface area decreased and SEI formation was suppressed with increasing heat-treatment temperature. It is also possible that electrical conductivity might improve or functional group content might decrease, resulting in a decrease of the amount of Li trapped in HC. Although further investigation on the 1st charge capacity and Coulombic efficiency is needed in future, we focus on discharge capacity hereafter in this study.

To estimate the theoretical capacity of each storage site of HC, the discharge capacities in each potential region were compared with the amounts of Li stored in each site estimated by the structural analysis. Because the 1st cycle discharge capacity was nearly the same as 2nd cycle charge capacity (Fig. S7 in the Supplementary Data), we believe that it is reasonable to discuss the relationship between discharge capacity and the reversible Li storage sites as discussed in other literatures [11–16,19,20].

At first, the high voltage capacity around 0.9–2.5 V is discussed. In the previous study, it has been reported that the H content has a good correlation with the discharge capacity of the 1-V plateau region [19]. So, the amount of Li corresponding to the H content was calculated by Eq. (1) assuming that H and Li react in a constant molar ratio, and compared to the capacity at 0.9–2.5 V.
Eq. 1
Q H = nFC H / 100 / 3.6
Here, Q H is the estimated capacity of edge H (mAh/g), n is the reaction molar ratio of Li to H, F is Faraday's constant (C/mol), C H is the H content (mass % or mol-H/100 g-active material) measured by elemental analysis, and 3.6 is the unit conversion factor from C to mAh. Fig. 5 (a) shows the heat-treatment temperature dependences of the estimated capacity, Q H, calculated by Eq. (1) when n = 0.5, 1, and 2 and the measured discharge capacity at 0.9–2.5 V. The Q H values were in good agreement with the measured discharge capacity when n = 1. This result shows that the discharge capacity in this voltage region is derived from the edge H, and H and Li react in an equimolar ratio. Incidentally, most of this H did not exist as oxygen-containing functional groups but as hydrogen terminations at the graphene edges, as discussed in Section 3.5. Though HC in this study also contains O, N, and S as revealed by elemental analysis and SEM-EDS, the amounts of these elements were very small comparing other reports on the effect of these elements [48–50].

Here, we discuss possible reactions of Li at the graphene edge. Many models for the reaction have been proposed, such as a model of Li adsorption on the graphene edge [13,19,40,41], a model of Li exchange to H sites [42] and a model of the formation of lithium-naphthalene complexes [38,43], as shown in Eqs. (2)–(6). Here, “C-C” represents the chemical bond between carbons, “C·” indicates the radical on carbon, and “C-H” shows hydrogen on the graphene edge (Eqs. (2–6)).
Eq. 2 C-C + 2Li+ + 2e− = 2C-Li
Eq. 3 C· + Li+ + e− = C-Li
Eq. 4 C-H + 2Li+ + 2e− = C-Li + LiH
Eq. 5 C-H + Li+ + e− = C-Li + 1/2H2
Eq. 6 C-H + Li+ + e− = [CH]- + Li +

Because Eq. (2) and Eq. (3) do not include the reaction with H, these equations are different from the Li storage reaction that is the focus of the present research. Furthermore, from the standpoint of the discharge amount, the reaction given by Eq. (3) results in little impact, as discussed below. As shown in Fig. 1(d), the radical concentrations in the HCs were about 1019 to 1020 spin/g, which can be converted to about 10−2 to 10−1 mmol/g in molar terms. Then, if all the radicals react with Li according to Eq. (3), the amount of Li can be calculated as 1 to 10 mAh/g, which is much less than the discharge amount at 0.9–2.5 V (100–400 mAh/g). Thus, it is unlikely that radicals afforded this discharge capacity. Eq. (4) is a reaction that consumes twice as many electrons as H, and thus is inconsistent with the above-mentioned result that the discharge capacity is proportional to the H content in an equimolar ratio of H and Li. Eq. (5) means a reaction emitting H2 gas. Because the discharge capacity of the 0.9–2.5 V region can also be observed in the 2nd cycle (Fig. S7 in the Supplementary Data), an irreversible reaction such as Eq. (5) is improbable. Therefore, a model in which Li adsorbs near edge H, as shown in Eq. (6), is most likely. The shift of the broad peak observed in 1H NMR might suggest the formation of [CH]- near Li+ in Eq. (6). As mentioned in Section 3.8, although a model of the formation of lithium-naphthalene complexes has been proposed [38,43], an obvious peak of lithium-naphthalenide was not detected in the 7Li NMR in this study.

In this section, we discuss the middle voltage capacity at 0.2–0.9 V. Based on the results of 7Li MAS NMR at room temperature, the discharge capacity of 0.2–0.9 V was considered to be attributable to Li storage in the interlayer spaces. The amount of Li stored in the interlayer spaces can be calculated as follows. In graphite, Li can be intercalated and deintercalated in the interlayer spaces of the graphene sheets, and the theoretical capacity of graphite is calculated as 372 mAh/g from LiC6 stoichiometry. On the other hand, HC had a broad XRD profile (Fig. 1(a)), suggesting that HC had a microcrystalline structure consisting of 70–120 carbon atoms and 4 to 5 stacked layers. The amount of Li stored in the interlayer spaces of the microcrystalline structure, Q int, can be calculated by Eq. (7) [22,51]. This equation consists of theoretical capacity of graphite, 372 mAh/g, and the correction term of small crystalline size.
Eq. 7
Q int
= 372

Here, a 0, c 0 are the lattice constants of the a- and c-axes, and L
a , and L
c are the crystalline sizes along the layer sheet direction and the stacking direction, respectively. The Q int values were calculated from Eq. (7) using the values listed in Table 2.

The calculated Q int values and the discharge capacities at 0.2–0.9 V are compared in Fig. 5(b). Calculated Q int values were about 220 mAh/g, increasing slightly with heat treat temperature. This result suggests that HC can store at most about 220 mAh/g in the interlayer spaces because of the limited development of the graphitic structure in HC. However, the measured discharge capacity of the 0.2–0.9 V region decreased slightly with increasing heat-treatment temperature, although the estimated Q int values did not decrease. A possible reason for this discrepancy could be that the size distribution of the crystallite structure or the interlayer spaces might influence the value of Q int.

Here, we discuss the low voltage capacity around 0–0.2 V. According to the results of the 7Li MAS NMR measurements, the discharge capacity of 0–0.2 V was considered to be attributable to Li storage in pores. Here, the amount of Li stored in pores, Q pore, has been reported as Eq. (8) [22]. This equation was derived by calculating the amount of vacancies from the density of HC, ρ, and theoretical density of graphite, ρ i, correcting the amount of vacancies by the structure parameters of crystallite, and assuming that the vacancies are fully occupied by pseudo-metallic Li and supposing that crystal structure of pseudo-metallic Li is body-centered cubic.
Eq. 8
Q pore

Here, the values with the superscript i are those of ideal graphite, that is, ρ i = 2.265 g/cm3, c 0 i = 6.708 Å, and a 0 i = 2.456 Å. Q pore was calculated using the values listed in Table 2 and compared to the experimental discharge capacity of 0–0.2 V in Fig. 5(c). As shown in Fig. 5(c), the calculated discharge capacity of pores, Q pore, was much higher than the measured discharge capacity of 0–0.2 V, and furthermore, their heat-treatment temperature dependences were opposite. This result means that Eq. (8) cannot appropriately estimate the amount of Li stored in pores.

Therefore, to consider the change of pore size by heat treatment, the pore size distribution, f pore(x), was calculated from the SAXS profile as a function of the pore size, x. The absolute volume of pores, V pore, was calculated from Eq. (9) [22] and used to correct the vertical axis of the pore size distribution.

The corrected pore size distribution profile is shown in Fig. 6 (a). As the heat-treatment temperature increased, the pore volume decreased (Fig. 6(a)), but the pore diameter increased (Fig. 1(c)). This result suggests the possibility that there is an optimum pore size for Li storage. Therefore, supposing that Li can be stored in pores of a specific size, the volume of pores whose diameter, x, was larger than a threshold size, d, was calculated as the integral value of the pore size distribution (namely, the gray-colored area shown in Fig. 6(b)). Eq. (8) was corrected by calculating the amount of pseudo-metallic Li stored in pores whose diameter is larger than d, and the capacity of pores, Q'pore was obtained as Eq. (10).
Eq. 10

The discharge capacity of pores, Q'pore, estimated from Eq. (10) with various d values is shown in Fig. 6(c). The measured discharge capacities at 0–0.2 V are also shown in Fig. 6(c). In the case of d = 0 or 0.6 nm, Q'pore decreased as the heat-treatment temperature increased, but showed an increasing tendency for d = 0.9–1.5 nm. When d = 1.2 nm, Q'pore was in good agreement with the measured discharge capacity at 0–0.2 V. This result suggests that the Li storage site of HC is pores whose size is larger than about 1.2 nm.

Although it has been reported that enlargement of the pore size by high-temperature heat treatment is effective for increasing the Li storage capacity close to 0 V [16], in this study, the quantitative pore size suitable for Li storage is suggested.

The measured discharge capacities at 0–0.2 V, 0.2–0.9 V, and 0.9–2.5 V, together with the estimated Li storage capacities of pores, interlayer spaces, and edge H are shown in Fig. 7 . At each heat-treatment temperature, the discharge capacities of 0–0.2 V, 0.2–0.9 V, and 0.9–2.5 V showed reliable agreement with the estimated results for the pores, interlayer spaces, and edge H, respectively. This result suggests that each range corresponds to the equilibrium potential of the Li storage reaction in each Li storage site. With increasing heat-treatment temperature, the capacity of 0–0.2 V (corresponding to pores) increases, the capacity of 0.2–0.9 V (corresponding to the interlayer spaces) decreases slightly, and the capacity of 0.9–2.5 V (corresponding to edge H) decreases remarkably. In other words, the decrease of total discharge capacity associated with high-temperature heat treatment is predominantly attributable to the decrease in edge H.

Pitch-derived HC was prepared by varying the heat-treatment temperature, and the structure of the obtained HC was analyzed. Based on the results of the structural analysis and charge/discharge curve analysis, the Li storage sites and theoretical storage capacity of HC were examined, and the following conclusions were obtained.
•
The dQ/dV analysis indicated that the discharge curve can be divided into three regions (0–0.2 V, 0.2–0.9 V, and 0.9–2.5 V).
•
The discharge capacity of the 0.9–2.5 V region was proportional to the H content, and was considered to originate from edge H. The peak shift of 1H NMR during the charge/discharge process suggested an interaction between Li and edge H.
•
The discharge capacity at 0.2–0.9 V agreed relatively well with the amount of Li stored in interlayer spaces.
•
The discharge capacity at 0–0.2 V was in good agreement with the amount of Li in pores larger than 1.2 nm, suggesting that suitable pores for Li storage are relatively large, and especially are pores larger than 1.2 nm.
•
The Li storage capacity at 0–0.2 V (corresponding to pore capacity) increased with increasing heat-treatment temperature, but the capacity at 0.2–0.9 V (interlayer capacity) was almost independent of the temperature. The capacity at 0.9–2.5 V (edge H capacity) showed a noticeable decrease with increasing heat-treatment temperatures. The decrease in the total discharge capacity of HC prepared at high heat-treatment temperatures was considered to be due to the large impact of the decrease in Li storage capacity in the 0.9–2.5 V region.

Based on the results obtained in this study, in future, the critical process parameters to control the amount of edge H and pores whose size is larger than 1.2 nm will be investigated, to achieve higher discharge capacity of HC. Investigation on discharge curve and Coulombic efficiency is also future work to clarify the Li storage mechanism and practical use of HC. In addition, the cyclic, high rate long cyclic, and rate tests are also crucial, to investigate the contribution and degradation of each Li storage site.
